title: Sudden collapse without warning

chart.oneLiner: 78M presents after a sudden loss of consciousness with NO warning, on a background of recurrent near-fainting and fatigue.

opening: I just dropped — no warning at all. One second I was fine, then I woke up on the floor.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 78-year-old man, now alert but fatigued. Good historian.

HPI: ABRUPT loss of consciousness with NO prodrome (no nausea, no warmth, no lightheadedness beforehand). Quick recovery without confusion. Several recent episodes of near-fainting and increasing fatigue, exertional tiredness, and a 'slow pulse' noticed by his home BP cuff. NO chest pain, NO seizure activity, NO incontinence. The absence of any warning is a red flag for a cardiac cause.
PMH: hypertension, coronary artery disease, prior MI. MEDS: metoprolol, lisinopril, aspirin, atorvastatin. SOCIAL: former smoker.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: alert, fatigued-appearing.
- Vitals: BP 104/64, HR ~38 and regular, RR 16, 36.9°C, SpO2 97% RA.
- Cardiac: BRADYCARDIC, regular; intermittent CANNON A-waves in the JVP; variable intensity of S1 (signs of AV dissociation) if specifically examined.
- Neuro: no focal deficit, oriented.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Emphasize (only if asked about prodrome/warning) that there was NO warning before the collapse.
- The bradycardia, cannon a-waves, and variable S1 are revealed only if examined.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.Troponin: Mildly elevated, stable — no acute infarct pattern.
labs.BMP: Na 139, K 4.6 (not hyperkalemic), Cr 1.1, Mg normal.
labs.Echo: Echo (later): prior inferior wall motion abnormality, preserved-to-mildly-reduced EF, no critical valve lesion.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Third-degree (complete) AV block: P waves and QRS complexes are dissociated; the atrial rate is faster than and independent of a slow ventricular escape rhythm.
ekg.verify: From the LITFL 'Complete Heart Block (3rd degree AV block)' library page — download an example tracing that matches: Third-degree (complete) AV block: P waves and QRS complexes are dissociated; the atrial ra … and self-host with credit to litfl.com.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.
ekg.imageDescription: Third-degree (complete) AV block: P waves and QRS complexes are dissociated; the atrial rate is faster than and independent of a slow ventricular escape rhythm.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Enlarged cardiac silhouette (cardiothoracic ratio >0.5) on a PA film; lungs otherwise clear.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: Enlarged cardiac silhouette (cardiothoracic ratio >0.5) on a PA film; lungs otherwise clear. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Cardiac syncope due to bradyarrhythmia / high-grade AV block (leading & CANT-MISS,9). Tachyarrhythmia (VT) (CANT-MISS,4). Structural cardiac disease/aortic stenosis (CANT-MISS,3). Vasovagal (much less likely — NO prodrome) (core,2). Seizure (core,2). Recognizes that abrupt syncope without prodrome + bradycardia points to a conduction problem (core,5).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Cardiac syncope due to bradyarrhythmia / high-grade AV block. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Tachyarrhythmia (VT); Structural cardiac disease/aortic stenosis. Also reasonable: Vasovagal (much less likely — NO prodrome); Seizure.

criticalActions.item: Cardiac syncope due to bradyarrhythmia / high-grade AV block

coreActions.item: Recognizes that abrupt syncope without prodrome + bradycardia points to a conduction problem

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: EKG immediately (CANT-MISS — diagnostic,6). Continuous telemetry / cardiac monitoring (CANT-MISS,4). Troponin (2). BMP incl. potassium/magnesium and review for AV-nodal-blocking drugs (2). TSH (1). PENALTY: discharging or failing to place the patient on a monitor (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG immediately; Continuous telemetry / cardiac monitoring.

penalties.item: discharging or failing to place the patient on a monitor

contraindicatedTests: discharging or failing to place the patient on a monitor

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings (be specific about the rhythm).
steps.rubric: Findings = third-degree (complete) AV block with AV dissociation and a slow ventricular escape. CREDIT: identifies bradycardia (3); recognizes P waves and QRS are DISSOCIATED / independent (6); names complete/third-degree AV block (4); systematic read (2). PENALTY: misreading as sinus bradycardia or 1st/2nd-degree block (-3).
keyFindings: recognizes P waves and QRS are DISSOCIATED / independent
criticalFindings: Third-degree (complete) AV block: P waves and QRS complexes are dissociated; the atrial rate is faster than and independent of a slow ventricular escape rhythm.
commonMistakes: misreading as sinus bradycardia or 1st/2nd-degree block
steps.idealAnswer: Third-degree (complete) AV block: P waves and QRS complexes are dissociated; the atrial rate is faster than and independent of a slow ventricular escape rhythm.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: Third-degree (complete) AV block: P waves and QRS complexes are dissociated; the atrial rate is faster than and independent of a slow ventricular escape rhythm.

coreActions.item: recognizes P waves and QRS are DISSOCIATED / independent

penalties.item: misreading as sinus bradycardia or 1st/2nd-degree block

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = cardiomegaly (cardiothoracic ratio >0.5) on an adequate PA film; lungs otherwise clear. CREDIT: identifies the enlarged cardiac silhouette / measures the cardiothoracic ratio (6); confirms an adequate PA projection and otherwise clear lungs (4). PENALTY: calling the film normal / missing the cardiomegaly (-3).
keyFindings: identifies cardiomegaly on a PA film
commonMistakes: calling a normal heart enlarged on an AP/portable film
steps.idealAnswer: Cardiomegaly with a cardiothoracic ratio >0.5 on an adequate PA film.

coreActions: identifies cardiomegaly on a PA film

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Given the EKG, what is your diagnosis and why is this dangerous?
steps.rubric: Complete (third-degree) heart block causing cardiac syncope (6). Explains the risk — unreliable escape rhythm, risk of asystole/sudden death (4).
steps.idealAnswer: Complete (third-degree) heart block causing cardiac syncope. Dangerous because the ventricular escape rhythm is slow and unreliable, with risk of prolonged asystole, hemodynamic collapse, and sudden death — it requires urgent monitoring and pacing.

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH syncope / pacing: continuous telemetry monitoring (CANT-MISS,4); transcutaneous/transvenous pacing for unstable bradycardia (CANT-MISS,6); cardiology consult for permanent pacemaker (CANT-MISS,5); atropine as temporizing (3); hold AV-nodal blocking agents (3); treat reversible causes (2). PENALTY: discharging complete heart block (-4); giving more AV-nodal blockers (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Telemetry and continuous monitoring. For symptomatic/unstable bradycardia: atropine, then transcutaneous pacing, and a temporary transvenous pacer as a bridge. Hold AV-nodal blocking agents. Cardiology consult for permanent pacemaker (definitive therapy). Treat reversible causes (ischemia, electrolytes, drug toxicity).

physicalExamMappings.finding: Cardiac: BRADYCARDIC, regular; intermittent CANNON A-waves in the JVP; variable intensity of S1 (signs of AV dissociation) if specifically examined.

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 78M presents after a sudden loss of consciousness with NO warning, on a background of recurrent near-fainting and fatigue. The diagnosis is Complete heart block, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Cardiac: BRADYCARDIC, regular; intermittent CANNON A-waves in the JVP; variable intensity of S1 (signs of AV dissociation) if specifically examined.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Complete heart block while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Complete heart block and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.
criticalInterpretationItems: Third-degree (complete) AV block: P waves and QRS complexes are dissociated; the atrial rate is faster than and independent of a slow ventricular escape rhythm.
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Third-degree (complete) AV block: P waves and QRS complexes are dissociated; the atrial rate is faster than and independent of a slow ventricular escape rhythm.

imageInterpretationPearls: An incidental abnormality is an interpretation task in its own right — name it, then judge its relevance.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
